elements.sent-2411-001: The work described in this paper builds on the polyhedral semantics of the Spatial Logic for Closure Spaces (\slcs) and the geometric spatial model checker PolyLogicA.
elements.sent-2411-002: Polyhedral models are central in domains that exploit mesh processing, such as 3D computer graphics.
elements.sent-2411-003: A discrete representation of polyhedral models is given by cell poset models, which are amenable to geometric spatial model checking on polyhedral models using the logical language \slcsE, a weaker version of \slcs.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this work we show that the mapping from polyhedral models to cell poset models preserves and reflects \slcsE.
elements.sent-2411-005: We also propose weak simplicial bisimilarity on polyhedral models and weak \plm-bisimilarity on cell poset models.
elements.sent-2411-006: Weak \plm-bisimilarity leads to a stronger reduction of models than its counterpart \plm-bisimilarity that was introduced in previous work.
elements.sent-2411-007: We show that the proposed bisimilarities enjoy the Hennessy-Milner property, i.e.\ two points are weakly simplicial bisimilar iff they are logically equivalent for~\slcsE.
elements.sent-2411-008: Similarly, two cells are weakly \plm-bisimilar iff they are logically equivalent in the poset-model interpretation of~\slcsE.
elements.sent-2411-009: Furthermore we present a procedure, and prove that it correctly computes the minimal model with respect to weak \plm-bisimilarity, i.e. with respect to logical equivalence of ~\slcsE.
elements.sent-2411-010: The procedure works via an encoding into \lts{s and then exploits branching bisimilarity on those LTSs.}
elements.sent-2411-011: This allows one to use in the implementation the minimization capabilities as included in the mCRL2 toolset.
elements.sent-2411-012: Various experiments are included to show the effectiveness of the approach.
elements.sent-2411-013: Spatial and spatio-temporal model checking have recently been successfully employed in a variety of application areas, including Collective Adaptive Systems~\cite{Ci+16a,Ci+18,AguzziAV24,AudritoDT24 , signal analysis~\cite{Ne+18 , image analysis~\cite{Ci+16,Ha+15,Ba+20 and polyhedral modelling~\cite{Be+22,Ci+23c,Be+24,Be+24a .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-014: Interest in these methods for spatial analysis is increasing in Computer Science and in other domains, including initially unanticipated ones, such as medical imaging~\cite{Be+19,Be+21 .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Spatial model checking is a global technique: it comprises the automatic verification of properties, expressed in a suitable spatial logic, such as the Spatial Logic for Closure Spaces (\slcs)~\cite{Ci+14,Ci+16 , on each point of a spatial model.}
elements.sent-2411-016: The logic \slcs{ has been defined originally for closure models, i.e. models based on \v{C ech closure spaces~\cite{Cec66 , a generalisation of topological spaces, and model checking algorithms have been developed for finite closure models also in combination with discrete time, leading to spatio-temporal model checking~\cite{Ci+18 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-017: The spatial model checker \voxlogica, proposed in~\cite{Be+19 , is very efficient in checking properties of large images -- represented as symmetric finite closure models -- expressed in~\slcs{ \cite{Be+19,Be+19a,Be+21 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-018: For example, the automatic segmentation via a suitable \slcs{ formula characterising the white matter of the brain in a 3D MRI image consisting of circa 12M voxels (i.e.\ $256 \times 256 \times 181$), requires approximately 10~seconds, using \voxlogica{ on a desktop computer~\cite{Be+19a .\footnote{Intel Core i9-9900K processor (with 8 cores and 16 threads) and 32GB of RAM\@.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-019: Note that \voxlogica{ checks such logical specifications for every point in the model exploiting parallel execution, memoization, and state-of-the-art imaging libraries~\cite{Be+19 .}}
elements.sent-2411-020: In~\cite{Ci+22,Ci+23 several bisimulations for finite closure spaces have been studied, with the aim to improve the efficiency of model checking via model minimisation.}
elements.sent-2411-021: These notions cover a spectrum from \cm-bisimilarity, an equivalence based on {\em proximity --- similar to and inspired by topo-bisimilarity for topological models~\cite{vBB07 --- to \cmc-bisimilarity, its specialisation for quasi-discrete closure models, and \copa-bisimilarity, an equivalence based on {\em conditional reachability .}}}
elements.sent-2411-022: Each of these bisimilarities has been equipped with its logical characterisation.
elements.sent-2411-023: The spatial model checking techniques mentioned above, targeting grid-based structures, have been extended to polyhedral models~\cite{Be+22,LoQ23 .}
elements.sent-2411-024: Polyhedra are subsets in~$\reals^n$ generated by simplicial complexes, i.e.\ finite collections of simplexes satisfying certain conditions.
elements.sent-2411-025: A simplex is the convex hull of a set of affinely independent points.
elements.sent-2411-026: Given a set $\ap$ of proposition letters, a polyhedral model is obtained from a polyhedron by assigning a polyhedral subset to each proposition letter $p \in \ap$, namely those points that ``satisfy'' proposition $p$.
elements.sent-2411-027: Polyhedral models in~$\reals^3$ can be used for (approximately) representing objects in continuous 3D~space.
elements.sent-2411-028: This is typical of many 3D~visual computing techniques, where an object is split into suitable geometric parts of different size.
elements.sent-2411-029: Such ways of splitting of an object are known as mesh techniques and include triangular surface meshes or tetrahedral volume meshes (see~\cite{LevinePRZ2012 ).}
elements.sent-2411-030: Interestingly, polyhedral models can conveniently be represented by discrete structures, the so-called cell poset models: each point of the polyhedron is mapped to a (unique) ``cell'', i.e.\ an element of the associated cell poset model.
elements.sent-2411-031: Cell poset models, being a particular case of Kripke models, are amenable to discrete model-checking.
elements.sent-2411-032: In~\cite{Be+22 a version of \slcs{ , called \slcsG\ in the sequel, has been proposed for expressing spatial properties of points lying in polyhedral models, and, in particular, conditional reachability properties.}}
elements.sent-2411-033: Besides negation and conjunction, the particular logic provides the $\gamma$~reachability operator.
elements.sent-2411-034: Informally, a point~$x$ in a polyhedral model satisfies the conditional reachability formula $\gamma(\form_1,\form_2)$ if there is a topological path starting from~$x$, ending in a point~$y$ satisfying $\form_2$, and such that all the intermediate points of the path between $x$ and~$y$ satisfy~$\form_1$.
elements.sent-2411-035: Note that neither $x$ nor~$y$ is required to satisfy~$\form_1$.
elements.sent-2411-036: Many other interesting properties, such as proximity (in the topological sense, i.e. ``being in the topological closure of'') or ``being surrounded by'' can be expressed using reachability (see~\cite{Be+22 ).}
elements.sent-2411-037: Moreover, in~\cite{Be+22 {\em simplicial bisimilarity has been proposed for (points of) polyhedral models and it has been shown that it enjoys the Hennessy-Milner Property (HMP) with respect to \slcsG{, and a geometric model checking algorithm as well as its related model checker, \polylogica{, have been presented.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-038: In~\cite{Ci+23c {\em \plm-bisimilarity has been proposed for (cells of) cell poset models, that also enjoyed the HMP with respect to \slcsG.}}
elements.sent-2411-039: In this paper we introduce a weaker version of conditional reachability, denoted by~$\eta$.
elements.sent-2411-040: A point~$x$ in a polyhedral model satisfies the conditional reachability formula $\eta(\form_1,\form_2)$ if there is a topological path starting from~$x$, ending in a point~$y$ satisfying~$\form_2$, and $x$~and all the intermediate points of the path between $x$ and~$y$ satisfy~$\form_1$.
elements.sent-2411-042: The operator $\eta$ can easily be expressed using~$\gamma$ and we will show that the logic where $\gamma$~has been replaced by~$\eta$ (\slcsE, in the sequel), is strictly weaker than \slcsG, in the sense that it distinguishes fewer points than~\slcsG.
elements.sent-2411-043: Furthermore, as mentioned above, \slcsG~can express proximity --- that boils down to the standard possibility modality~$\Diamond$ in the poset model interpretation --- whereas \slcsE{ ~cannot.}
elements.sent-2411-044: Nevertheless, many interesting reachability properties can be expressed in \slcsE{ and, more importantly, the latter characterises bisimilarities (in the polyhedral model and the associated poset model) that are coarser than simplicial bisimilarity and \plm-bisimilarity, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-045: We show that \slcsE{ can be interpreted on cell poset models so that the mapping from a polyhedral model into its cell poset model preserves and reflects the logic: a point satisfies a formula of~\slcsE{ if and only if the cell which it is mapped to satisfies the formula\footnote{A similar feature was shown to hold for \slcsG\ in~\cite{Be+22 .. .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-046: This result paves the way to the definition and implementation of model checking techniques for \slcsE{ on polyhedral models, by working on their discrete representations.}
elements.sent-2411-047: In this paper we introduce {\em weak simplicial bisimilarity on polyhedral models ($\wsibis$) and show that it enjoys the HMP with respect to \slcsE, i.e.\ $\wsibis$ coincides with logical equivalence $\slcsEeq$ as induced by \slcsE{.}}
elements.sent-2411-048: As in the case of traditional (temporal) model checking, efficiency of spatial model checking can be improved by suitable model minimisation techniques.
elements.sent-2411-049: In particular, we are interested in techniques based on {spatial bisimilarity.}
elements.sent-2411-050: We propose a notion of bisimulation equivalence for cell poset models, namely weak \plm-bisimilarity ($\wplmbis$, to be read as \lq{ weak plus-minus\rq{ bisimilarity) such that two points in the polyhedral model are weakly simplicial bisimilar if and only if their cells are weakly \plm-bisimilar.}}
elements.sent-2411-051: We show that on cell poset models $\wplmbis$ coincides with $\slcsEeq$.
elements.sent-2411-052: Building upon these theoretical results we introduce a minimisation procedure based on weak \plm-bisimilarity, namely {\em weak \plm-minimisation .}
elements.sent-2411-053: The procedure uses an encoding of cell poset models into labelled transition systems (\lts{s ) following an approach that is similar to that presented in~\cite{Ci+23a for finite closure models.}}
elements.sent-2411-054: More precisely, in the case of cell poset models, there is a one-to-one correspondence between the states of the \lts{ and the cells of the poset model.}
elements.sent-2411-055: It is shown that two cells are weakly \plm-bisimilar in the poset model if and only if they are --- as states of the encoded \lts{ --- branching bisimulation equivalent.}
elements.sent-2411-056: This provides an effective way for computing the equivalence classes for the set of cells, from which the minimal model is built, on which \slcsE{ model checking can be safely performed.}
elements.sent-2411-057: Efficient \lts{ minimisation tools are available for branching bisimulation, such as the one provided by the \mcrl\ toolset~\cite{Gr+17 .}}
elements.sent-2411-058: As we will see in Section~\ref{sec:Experiments , this can bring to a drastic reduction of the size of the spatial model, thus increasing the practical efficiency of spatial model checking.}
elements.sent-2411-059: For instance, Figure~\ref{fig:LcubeLTS shows a large example of a cube structure, composed of 6,154 cells of three colours: white, green and grey.}
elements.sent-2411-060: This model is reduced to an LTS consisting of only 38 states, which is a reduction of two orders of magnitude.
elements.sent-2411-061: The different white, green and grey states of the minimised LTS represent the various equivalence classes of cells in the original polyhedral model.
elements.sent-2411-062: Even if this is a synthetic example, chosen on purpose for its symmetry properties, it illustrates the potential of the approach.
elements.sent-2411-063: Figure~\ref{fig:LcubeLTS only gives a first visual impression of spatial minimisation for polyhedra.}
elements.sent-2411-064: We postpone the discussion of the details to Section~\ref{sec:Experiments .}
elements.sent-2411-065: Summarising, the main original contributions in this paper are:.
elements.sent-2411-066: Presentation of \slcsE{, a spatial logic for polyhedral models which is weaker than \slcsG{;.}}
elements.sent-2411-067: Introduction of {\em weak simplicial bisimilarity on polyhedral models ($\wsibis$) and showing that it enjoys the HMP with respect to \slcsE{;.}}
elements.sent-2411-068: Introduction of {\em weak \plm-bisimilarity on cell poset models ($\wplmbis$), with the corresponding HMP result;.}
elements.sent-2411-069: Introduction of a novel cell poset model minimisation procedure based on weak \plm-bisimilarity, including the formal proof of its correctness;.
elements.sent-2411-070: Proof-of-concept of the practical potential and effectiveness of this approach through a prototype toolchain and spatial model checking examples.
elements.sent-2411-071: It is shown that the cell poset models can be drastically reduced by several orders of magnitude.
elements.sent-2411-072: The paper is structured as follows.
elements.sent-2411-073: We provide a summary of necessary background information in Section~\ref{sec:BackAndNotat .}
elements.sent-2411-074: Section~\ref{sec:slcsE introduces~\slcsE{ and addresses its relationship with~\slcsG.}}
elements.sent-2411-075: It is also shown that \slcsE{ is preserved and reflected by the mapping~$\map$ from polyhedral models to finite poset models.}
elements.sent-2411-076: Weak simplicial bisimilarity and weak \plm-bisimilarity are defined in Section~\ref{sec:WeakBis where it is also shown that they enjoy the HMP with respect to the interpretation of \slcsE{ on polyhedral models and on finite poset models, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-077: The minimisation procedure, based on weak \plm-bisimilarity and exploiting its relationship with branching bisimulation equivalence, is defined in Section~\ref{sec:EtaMinimisation where its correctness is also addressed.}
elements.sent-2411-078: The procedure is currently implemented by means of an experimental toolchain using \mcrltwo{ and is discussed in Section~\ref{sec:toolchain .}}
elements.sent-2411-079: Examples of use of the toolchain are presented in Section~\ref{sec:Experiments .}
elements.sent-2411-080: Conclusions and a discussion on future work are reported in Section~\ref{sec:ConclusionsFW .}
elements.sent-2411-081: Finally, in Appendix~\ref{apx:DetailedProofs the proofs for some auxiliary lemmas are provided and, in Appendix~\ref{apx:AdditionalExamples , some additional minimisation examples are shown.}}

elements.sent-2411-094: Below we recall some basic notions, assuming that the reader is familiar with topological spaces, Kripke models, and posets.
elements.subsection-2411-001: Polyhedral Models and Cell Poset Models
elements.sent-2411-095: A simplex $\sigma$ of dimension $d$ is the convex hull of a set $\SET{\mathbf{v_0 ,\ldots, \mathbf{v_d }$ of $d+1$~affinely independent points in~$\reals^m$, with $d \leq m$, i.e.\ $\sigma = \ZET{ \lambda_0\mathbf{v_0 + \ldots + \lambda_d\mathbf{v_d {\lambda_0,\ldots,\lambda_d \in [0,1]\mbox{ and \sum_{i=0 ^{d \lambda_i = 1}$.}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-097: Any subset of the set $\SET{\mathbf{v_0 ,\ldots, \mathbf{v_d }$ of points characterising a simplex~$\sigma$ induces a simplex~$\sigma'$ in turn, and we write $\sigma' \sqsubseteq \sigma$, noting that $\sqsubseteq$~is a partial order, e.g.\ $A \sqsubseteq A \sqsubseteq AB$, $B \sqsubseteq B \sqsubseteq AB$ and $AB \sqsubseteq AB$.}}
elements.sent-2411-098: The {\em barycentre $b_{\sigma}$ of $\sigma$ is defined as follows: $ b_{\sigma = \sum_{i=0 ^d \frac{1 {d+1}\mathbf{v_i $.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-099: The relative interior $\relint{\sigma}$ of a simplex~$\sigma$ is the same as $\sigma$ ``without its borders'', i.e.\ the set $\ZET{ \lambda_0\mathbf{v_0 + \ldots + \lambda_d\mathbf{v_d {\lambda_0,\ldots,\lambda_d \in (0,1]\mbox{ and \sum_{i=0 ^{d \lambda_i = 1}$.}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-106: A simplicial complex~$K$ is a finite collection of simplexes of~$\reals^m$ such that: (i) if $\sigma \in K$ and $\sigma' \sqsubseteq \sigma$ then also $\sigma' \in K$; (ii) if $\sigma, \sigma' \in K$ and $\sigma \cap \sigma' \not=\emptyset$, then $\sigma \cap \sigma' \sqsubseteq \sigma$ and $\sigma \cap \sigma' \sqsubseteq \sigma'$.

elements.sent-2411-147: The satisfaction relation for $\gamma(\form_1, \form_2)$, for a polyhedral model $\calP = (|K|,\peval{\calP )$ and $x\in |K|$, as defined in~\cite{Be+22 , is recalled below:\\begin{equation}0.5em] $ {l c l} \calP, x \models \gamma(\form_1,\form_2) \Leftrightarrow a topological path \pi: [0,1] \to |K| exists such that \pi(0)=x, \calP, \pi(1) \models \form_2, and \calP, \pi(r) \models \form_1 for all r\in \!\!(0,\!1).  $ [0.5em] We also recall the interpretation of \slcsG{} on poset models. The satisfaction relation for $\gamma(\form_1, \form_2)$, for a poset model $\calF=(W,\preccurlyeq,\peval{\calF})$ and $w\in W$, is as follows: [0.5em] $ {l c l} \calF, w \models \gamma(\form_1,\form_2) \Leftrightarrow a \plm-path \pi: [0;\ell] \to W exists such that \pi(0)=w, \calF, \pi(\ell) \models \form_2, and \calF, \pi(i) \models \form_1 for all i\in \!\!(0;\!\ell).  $ [0.5em] In~\cite{Be+22} it has also been shown that, for all $x \in |K|$ and \slcsG{} formulas~$\form$, we have: $\calP,x \models \form$ if and only if $\map(\calP),\map(x) \models \form$. In addition, simplicial bisimilarity, a novel notion of bisimilarity for polyhedral models, has been defined that uses a subclass of topological paths and it has been shown to enjoy the classical Hennessy-Milner property: two points $x_1,x_2 \in |K|$ are simplicial bisimilar, written $x_1 \sibis^{\calP} x_2$, if and only if they satisfy the same \slcsG{} formulas, i.e. they are equivalent with respect to the logic \slcsG{}, written $x_1 \slcsGeq^{\calP} x_2$. The result has been extended to \plm-bisimilarity on finite poset models, a notion of bisimilarity based on \plm-paths: $w_1,w_2 \in W$ are \plm-bisimilar, written $x_1 \plmbis^{\calF} x_2$, if and only if they satisfy the same \slcsG{} formulas, i.e. $x_1 \slcsGeq^{\calF} x_2$ (see~\cite{Ci+23c} for details). In summary, we have: \[ x_1 \sibis^{\calP} x_2 iff x_1 \slcsGeq^{\calP} x_2 iff \map(x_1) \slcsGeq^{\map(\calP)} \map(x_2) iff \map(x_1)\plmbis^{\map(\calP)} \map(x_2).\end{equation}.}}

elements.section-2411-005: Building the Minimal Model Modulo Logical Equivalence
elements.sent-2411-255: In this section we present a minimisation procedure for finite poset models modulo weak \plm-bisimilarity or, equivalently, modulo $\slcsEeq$.
elements.sent-2411-256: Given a finite poset model $\calF = (W, \preccurlyeq, \peval{\calF )$, the procedure consists of three steps: [0.5em] Step 1: The poset model~$\calF$ is encoded as an \lts{ denoted~$\posToltsC(\calF)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-257: The set of states of~$\posToltsC(\calF)$ is~$W\!$.
elements.sent-2411-258: The encoding is such that it is ensured that logically equivalent points are mapped to branching bisimilar states.
elements.sent-2411-259: Thus, for points $w_1, w_2 \in W$ that are logically equivalent with respect to \slcsE{ in the poset model~$\calF$, i.e.\ $w_1 \slcsEeq^{\calF w_2$, we have that they are branching bisimilar as states in the \lts~$\posToltsC(\calF)$, i.e.\ $w_1 \beq^{\posToltsC(\calF) w_2$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-260: Step 2: The \lts~$\posToltsC(\calF)$ is reduced modulo branching bisimilarity using available software tools, such as \mcrltwo~\cite{Gr+17 .}
elements.sent-2411-261: This step yields the set of equivalence classes of~$W$ for~$\beq^{\posToltsC(\calF)}$.
elements.sent-2411-262: Because of the correspondence of logical equivalence and branching bisimilarity, we obtain $W / {\slcsEeq^{\calF }$.}
elements.sent-2411-263: It turns out that this model is not necessarily a poset model (see the example in Fig.~\ref{fig:posetPMbisVar in Section~\ref{sec:Experiments ).}}
elements.sent-2411-264: However, it is a reflexive Kripke model of the form~$(W / {\slcsEeq^{\calF ,R,\peval{ )$ where $R$~is a relation induced by the ordering~$\preccurlyeq$ of~$\calF$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-265: We obtain the \lts~$\posToltsC(\calF)$ from the poset~$\calF$ as follows.
elements.sent-2411-266: Fig.~\ref{subfig:polyM shows an example of a simple polyhedral model~$\calP'$.}
elements.sent-2411-267: The \lts~$\posToltsC(\calF')$ associated with the poset model~$\calF'$ of~$\calP'$ is shown in Fig.~\ref{subfig:concreteLTS .\footnote{Note that $\tau$~self-loops in \lts{s are irrelevant since we are working modulo branching bisimilarity.}}}
elements.sent-2411-268: In this paper we focus mainly on correctness, while in future work we will address optimisation of the encoding procedures.

elements.sent-2411-387: In this section we provide a brief overview of an experimental toolchain to study the minimisation procedure for polyhedral models and to illustrate the practical potential of the theory presented in the previous section.
elements.sent-2411-388: The further development and a thorough analysis of the toolchain will be the subject of future work.
elements.sent-2411-389: Fig.~\ref{fig:toolchain illustrates the elements of the toolchain that, starting from a polyhedral model in~json format, produces the set of equivalence classes and the minimal Kripke model.}
elements.sent-2411-390: The former may serve as input for the \polyvisualizer{ tool\footnote{http://ggrilletti2.scienceontheweb.net/polyVisualizer/polyVisualizer\_static\_maze.html ~\cite{Be+22 , a polyhedra visualizer, to inspect the results, whereas the latter can be used for spatial model checking through an adapted version of \polylogica{ that can check spatial properties on Kripke models using \plm-paths instead of regular paths.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-439: Polyhedral models are widely used in domains that exploit mesh processing such as 3D computer graphics.
elements.sent-2411-440: These models are typically huge, consisting of very many cells.
elements.sent-2411-441: Spatial model checking of such models is an interesting, novel approach to verify properties of such models and visualise the results in a graphically appealing way.
elements.sent-2411-442: In previous work the polyhedral model checker \polylogica{ was developed for this purpose~\cite{Be+22 .}}
elements.sent-2411-443: In~\cite{Be+22 simplicial bisimilarity was proposed for polyhedral models --- i.e. models of continuous space --- while \plm-bisimilarity, the corresponding equivalence for cell-poset models --- discrete representations of polyhedral models --- was first introduced in~\cite{Ci+23c .}}
elements.sent-2411-444: In order to support large model reductions, in this paper the novel notions of weak simplicial bisimilarity and weak \plm-bisimilarity have been proposed, and the correspondence between the two has been studied.
elements.sent-2411-445: We have also proposed \slcsE{, a weaker version of the Spatial Logic for Closure Spaces on polyhedral models, and we have shown that simplicial bisimilarity enjoys the Hennessy-Milner property (Thm.~\ref{thm:HMPpoly ).}}
elements.sent-2411-446: Furthermore, we have proven that the property holds for \plm-bisimilarity on poset models and the interpretation of \slcsE{ on such models (Thm.~\ref{theo:PMbisEqSLCSEeq ).}}
elements.sent-2411-447: Model checking results can be visualised by projecting them onto the original polyhedral structure, showing in a specific colour all the cells satisfying the property of interest.
elements.sent-2411-448: In order to reduce model checking time and computing resources, we have proposed an effective procedure that computes the minimal model, modulo logical equivalence with respect to the logic \slcsE{, of a polyhedral model.}
elements.sent-2411-449: Such minimised models are also amenable to model checking with \polylogica{.}
elements.sent-2411-450: The procedure has been formalised and proven correct.
elements.sent-2411-451: A prototype implementation of the procedure has been developed in the form of a toolchain, that also involves operations provided by the \mcrl{ toolset, to study the practical feasibility of the approach and to identify possible bottlenecks.}
elements.sent-2411-452: We have also shown how the model checking results of the minimal model can be projected back onto the original polyhedral model.
elements.sent-2411-453: This provides a direct 3D visual inspection of the results through a polyhedra visualizer.
elements.sent-2411-454: In future work we aim at a more sophisticated implementation of the procedure, possibly using in a more direct way the minimisation operations provided by \mcrl{ and integrating the various steps in the procedure.}
elements.sent-2411-455: Furthermore, we would be interested in extending \slcsE{ with further operators, for example those concerning notions of distance, and applying the method on a larger number of case studies.}

elements.sent-2411-645: In particular, we are interested in a framework for model reduction with the following features:.
elements.sent-2411-646: It should be {\em sound , i.e. be based on a notion of bisimilarity that enjoys the Hennessy-Milner Property (HMP) so that completeness and correctness of the optimised model checking procedure --- via model reduction --- are guaranteed.}
elements.sent-2411-647: It should be {\em optimal in the sense of yielding an as small model as possible, yet a {\em useful one.}}
elements.sent-2411-648: In this respect we have been inspired by the use of branching bisimilarity in the context of LTSs: branching bisimilarity --- that is weaker than strong bisimilarity --- enjoys the HMP with respect to CTL$^*$ without $X$ (next) --- that is weaker than full CTL$^*$ --- and both the equivalence and its logical characterisation are widely used in concurrency theory and its applications.
elements.sent-2411-649: In essence, weak simplicial bisimilarity in the context of spatial logic is a re-interpretation in space of branching bisimilarity in the context of temporal logic.
elements.sent-2411-650: Similarly, \slcsE{ can be seen as the spatial counterpart of CTL$^* \setminus X$.}
elements.sent-2411-651: It should exploit {\em existing tools for minimisation via bisimulation.}

elements.sent-2411-653: As we mentioned above, the fact that logical equivalence $\slcsEeq$ is {\em coarser than $\slcsGeq$ implies that poset model minimisation modulo $\slcsEeq$ results in models that can be smaller than those obtained modulo $\slcsGeq$, and this is one reason why we focus on \slcsE{ in the present paper.}}
elements.sent-2411-654: Of course, we do not have a general measure of the ``gain'', in terms of percentage of reduction in the number of cells of the input models, when using $\slcsEeq$ instead of $\slcsGeq$, because this depends on the specific model.
